data.text:
INT: --of Mark Verstandig. Please continue your story. 

MV: Now the Gestapo officer understood Polish. He must have been a Volksdeutsche. So he started to smile. He said, look, Dr. Droba, I didn't come here to make trouble. Think about it. We'll see each other. He-- he gave to undersatnd that he want a bribe. 

The same day, we sent in somebody, a certain Mendel Bloch. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. He was really making trousers. But somehow he-- he had some connections with the Gestapo. He was a little-- he was-- he was-- he was a fixer. He went to this fellow and he came back, said he wanted curtains for his house. We-- he want-- and he gave him the measurements, and the curtains were made. 

And he's let it be known to Droba that Dr. Verstandig should not work otherwise as he did up till now. Should-- he-- he should not-- he-- he shouldn't be employed in any other-- in any other job as-- I mean, other than he's employed here. This way he gave to understand that I should continue the work. 

And that was almost the end of 1941, almost the end of 1941. I think December. 

Maybe it was-- it-- it was November. Anyhow, a few weeks later it was already December. It was a cold day. In the morning it was already dark. Dr. Droba's brother, Brigadier Joseph Droba, came, knocked at the door where I lived. He's-- opened the door. He said there is trouble. Run for your life. 

The Gestapo is here. Representative of the Justice Department here. They came to investigate that you are going-- coming to court. You're attending court cases every day, and you are conferring and sitting with the judges in the judges' chambers where other Polish lawyers are waiting in queue for five minutes, and so on and so on. There is trouble. We may all go to Auschwitz, and you must disappear. 

I was very-- I was very confused, and was very scared. He-- but I rushed after him, called him back. And said, look, I've got an idea. 

If the court personnel, the judges, and the secretaries, and the cleaners, will testify that I used to come to court but in my own cases, and not as a lawyer, everything could be saved. And they will find in the index my name many times, either as a plaintiff or as a-- or as a defendant. That-- that's a good idea. I told you about these fictitious cases which went into-- which went into the index. 

He said it's an excellent idea. We'll ask Dr. Frank. Maybe he could help too. And the fact was that they came to court. And after court about Dr. Verstandig coming to court every day, he said, no. Not every day. He used to come to court from time to time, but in his own cases, and not as a lawyer. Here's the index. 

They looked up the-- in the index, and that was not an index in the-- I mean, an artificial thing. It was a real index. Every few months, every few weeks, I had a case, my own case. And Dr. Frank-- I mentioned it last time about Dr. Frank. He was a [GERMAN], and he was a friend of Dr. Droba. And he was very friendly with me. He was there among this Gestapo, and he talked to them in German. 

He said, look, I've seen him many times here, but as-- in his own-- in his own-- in the capacity either of a defendant or in-- in some cases-- and they listened, and they very-- Dr. Frank, a [GERMAN], is-- is-- is-- is confirming what is written in the index. So they said, sorry. Must be a mistake. Somebody must have seen Dr. Verstandig coming to court and thought that he is representing, that he is-- that-- that he is working as a lawyer. And they went away. 

And in that way, the judges were saved. They could have gone-- the whole court could have gone to Auschwitz, including Droba. But the whole court could have been sent to Auschwitz. And that was what this young fellow Greenberg did. He did-- first of all he sent the Gestapo, and when the Gestapo-- when the Gestapo didn't do anything, he made-- he-- he denounced us. He denounced us to the Justice Department. 

To conclude this story, because I am talking about this fellow Greenberg, he got-- he got his punishment later. When Mielec was-- when-- was liquidated, and the whole town was expelled, part-- the young-- the-- the youngsters to concentration camps, to-- or to labor camps, and the older people to-- to-- not to Belzec, but to-- to-- to [NON-ENGLISH], and the [INAUDIBLE] to [NON-ENGLISH] on the way to build it. Belzec wasn't ready yet. Should have been-- Belzec wasn't ready yet. 

And some people just ran away, including myself. It looked that the brothers, the two brothers, Greenberg, Nachamia Greenberg and his elder brother, were hidden by [POLISH] for a few days. And after a few days-- we were already in Polaniec. After a few days, they reappeared in Staszow, a town about 20 kilometers or 18 kilometers from Polaniec. A larger-- a larger town, almost as big as Mielec. 

And there, they were walking. There was no ghetto there. The situation was like similar was before-- like-- like before that in Mielec, like before the expulsion. There was not a closed ghetto there. Now they were walking themselves nicely on the main street, and a Gestapo car passed. And in this Gestapo car was a Gestapo man called Jeck. 

This Jeck was a-- a-- he went-- he was a Volksdeutsche. And he was our colleague in the Mielec gymnasium. He was much younger than I am. I didn't even know him. But later he became an executioner, and his-- his hobby was to hunt down his colleagues, his Jewish colleagues. His Jewish colleagues. 

INT: Well, just stop for a moment there. OK, you're talking about Herr Jeck. 

MV: Yeah. And this Jeck spotted him, stopped the car, and what have you got in your-- and asked what have you got in your-- in-- in-- in your briefcase? Looked up the briefcase-- the briefcase, and there were dollars there. Said you come up with. And they took both brothers outside the town where they shot them. And that was the end of them. 

They collected thousands and thousands of dollars, which he had not only in the briefcases, but in-- hidden in-- in-- in-- in this-- money belts and so on. And people in Staszow said that later also the clothes was already torn by the Gestapo to look-- which-- who were looking for the dollars. They still found a number of banknotes, of hundred dollars banknotes amongst them. But this was the case-- this was the end of-- of this-- Nachamia Greenberg and his brother. 

So that was the case of my-- and-- and at that time I-- we-- December-- in December, 1942, I had to stop really for good going to Droba's office and going to court. 

INT: '41 or '42? 

MV: In '41. In '41 I stopped-- I stopped working for them. I started to work from my-- my private house. Not-- not a private house. I had a room-- I had a room not in my house, together with Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] who was the president of the Judenrat. He gave me one-- he had a large-- he had a large flat-- a large flat. He gave me one of his rooms. 

And from that room, I was preparing briefs for other lawyers, not only in Mielec but for other lawyers in [POLISH] and in other small towns. And I made more money working from home than even while I was-- while I was working with Droba where I was a partner. 

But I would like to tell you a very-- a very interesting period-- a very interesting case, which will show you the spirit of a Polish judge. 

A case-- a fellow came up to my place-- to-- to my-- to our-- to our office. I don't remember his name. He was-- he lived in-- not in town, but in a village. And he was a well-known horse trader. An expert in horses. A horse trader. 

And he had a case. He went to buy a horse from a-- a-- from an estate. And as he didn't know whether a Jew is allowed to buy a horse or not, he took a friend of his who was a German, a Volksdeutsche, in a neighboring village in German. He took with him that he is going to buy this horse in his name. 

So they went to this fellow. He bought the horse. And-- and-- and he didn't take him-- he didn't buy a horse like you see this-- also to sign a contract. But he said to the fellow, he is buying the horse. But he paid for the horse. And he took the horse with him. 

And a few days later, this German came to us. So he told him, look, it's such a fine horse. I don't think that you should keep this horse in your stable. It's not-- you know, you haven't-- you haven't got such a nice stable. Besides this, there are fellows around. They-- they will-- they will be jealous that you have got such a nice horse. I will keep this horse for you in my stable. 

So the Jewish fellow was suspicious. Said, look, a horse is a horse, and a stable is a stable. It's you-- I take the responsibility. Nothing will happen to this horse here. A few days later, he came with another few fellows and he removed the horse by force. He said it's my horse. 

And the fellow came to me and told me, look, he grabbed my horse. What should I do? I said, look, that's a hell of a-- you have a hell of a trouble because if you issue a litigation, you have to issue at the same time a German translation-- a German translation, and the government will take you-- the-- the government will take that. So said, doesn't matter. If the German government will take it, the government will take it. But I don't want him to take it. He is a cheat. He was a friend of mine, and he's a cheat. 

If you could make-- if you could make a-- if you could issue a writ against this German. I told him, look, I will have to ask Droba because it's a German involved. And Droba said, why not? A case is a case. Especially when you issue a German translation. So you issue a German translation, you are [INAUDIBLE]. 

So we issued the-- we issued the case against this-- against this German. And before the case, Droba told me, look, I am not going to represent this fellow in court. There is a lot of pressure behind the scenes. I think we should wash our hands of this case. We are not going to represent him. And we should tell him that he should drop it. 

We told him, drop it. We are not going to court. Said, I will go to court myself. I don't need a lawyer. If you don't want it, I'll go to somebody else. Nobody want it. So he went in by himself to court. The judge was Kosowski. Young judge from before the war. I knew him very well. A very nice man. 

And he brought in a-- the German brought in about 20 witnesses that he intended to buy the horse, that he borrowed money from this Jew, and he took the Jew only as an expert on horses. He should be an expert on a horse, so you buy a good horse. To look at the-- the legs and so on. An expert. I brought him as an expert. 

And all they said. We knew that he went to-- everything was-- 20 witnesses. And he had a lawyer. The name of the lawyer was Dr. [POLISH], who was an anti-Semite from before the war. But he never showed up openly. And he never said anything against me representing people in court. Also he knew that I am not allowed to. So he behaved quite all right. But we knew that he was always an antisemite. 

So the judge, Kosowski, called in Dr. [POLISH] to the-- to a-- to a-- to a conference. And he told him, look, this fellow hasn't got a lawyer. Nobody wants to represent him. I have got an idea, said Kosowski. 

Said, if you agree that this case should be stopped for-- this-- this-- kind of an agreement, we stop the case for three years. Now, the position is such. The case is stopped ad-- ad infinitum. If after three years it will not be started again, that's the end of it. This fellow, the German, has got the horse. Well, on three years' time, we will know what's going to happen. 

So this is-- you should agree that we should stop the case, and nothing will-- I mean, your-- your client should be happy because he will have the horse for the time being, for the next three years. But this [POLISH] sees that he had so many witnesses and so on. He said to him-- he said to the judge, I'm sorry, Your Honor, I want judgment. I am not agreeing to stop the case. I want a judgment in this case. 

So the judge put on his hat that was-- that was the procedure. This was the judge hat to-- to pronounce the judgment. And he said to him, [SPEAKING POLISH]. Means the plaintiff won the case. That's his horse. The plaintiff won the case. 

All the people left, and [POLISH] makes an appeal. And in this appeal, he wrote, no more, no less. That it is amazing that the judge issued a judgment like that against the evidence of 20 Christian-- of 20 Christian witnesses. Against the un-sworn evidence of the plaintiff-- un-sworn evidence of the plaintiff-- where it is known that the Jews are perjurering themselves in Polish court. 

And Kosowski had immediately called in [NON-ENGLISH], I think. The [NON-ENGLISH] or somebody else, and they photo-- they made a copy-- photocopy of this-- of this appeal. The appeal went to the higher court in Tarnow. The higher court send it back to Melbourne. And another judge didn't-- didn't need to issue a judgment because in the meantime, all the Jews were expelled or killed. Mielec was no more, and there were no more Jews. 

But that was not the end of it, because after the war the-- Judge Kosowski dug out the document, the appeal, where he said about the Jews and about him. And he submitted this-- this appeal to two instances, one to the bar that he should be struck out as a solicitor, the other to the prosecutor that he endangered the life of a Polish judge who-- who gave judgment in accordance with-- with his conscience by issuing a-- this. 

The end was that he was imprisoned for eight years, but he died in prison. This Dr. [POLISH] died in prison. So it shows you only about different kind of Poles. One was Judge Kosowski, who stuck out his neck in order to-- to issue a judgment in accordance with his conscience where he knew really that a-- he can pay with his life for it. Yet were a few cases-- about the court cases-- about the court cases in Mielec. 

INT: At this time, at the beginning of 1942 when you're working from home, what was the situation in Mielec with regard to the Jewish question? 

MV: The situation in Mielec hasn't changed. From the beginning-- I mean, from the beginning there were-- the beginning was very-- was-- was very cruel because the Germans burned down the synagogues, and together with the Jews-- and with Jews they pushed in over there. 

But later in Mielec, the Jewish life in Mielec was almost-- almost as the Jewish life-- as the Jewish life in Mielec before the war. Situation-- the only thing is you had to wear-- you had to wear a band showing that you are Jewish. But this-- I may just tell you an interesting story. 

We had an estate, a large estate, called [POLISH]. I will show you here the pictures of this manor house over there. 17 kilometers from Mielec. And mentioned to you that my father left-- when we left in [POLISH], he left my sister, [POLISH], Berta, he left her in charge of [POLISH]. 

After a few weeks, she was thrown out by a family called Muller. Very interesting family because the brother of this Mrs. Muller was an appeal judge in Krakow, Jeck, and he was a good friend of my father. In a high-- very high position, appeal court judge. 

And her nephew was a Gestapo man called Jeck who was-- whose hobby was killing Jews after Mielec was-- was-- after the Jews in Mielec were expelled. So they threw out my sister. In the beginning, they sent her some parcels to Mielec, but that was all. But later they stopped it too. 

When I came back from Przemysl, I studied-- I made a study of-- of this German law. On what ground they confiscated the [POLISH] and put in a [GERMAN], which means somebody-- a trustee, a [GERMAN]. And the [GERMAN] threw out my-- my-- my sister. I studied this law, and I came to the conclusion that the German Landrat-- which means the local authority-- broke the German law. He had no right to do it. 

And I decided that I am going to make an appeal to the government, to the general governor in Krakow that they broke the law, and my-- my-- my sister should be reinstated in [POLISH]. But I thought before I am going to send out this appeal, I will consult a solicitor, a certain Dr. Eisenberg. 

I mentioned, I think, Dr. Eisenberg before. He was an old lawyer, a very good lawyer, who originated from Galicia but studied in Germany-- studied in Vienna. Studied in Vienna. I knew that he-- that-- that he-- that he's a good lawyer, and he knew German. And I wrote this appeal in German. 

So I came-- I said I'll go to consult him. As a matter of fact, I went over there and I saw this Dr. Eisenberg, an older man, maybe he was 65 years old. To me it looked an old man. And he was-- I met him outside. He-- he didn't live in the Jewish quarter. He lived outside the Jewish quarter. And-- but there was no closed ghetto. You could live wherever you wanted. 

And I saw him with his arm band in the German-- in the star of David. And I looked at him. He was-- looked to me a very old man. It was the first time that I regretted coming back. In the beginning, I thought, oh, I'll wear-- I'll wear this band. But I could see-- I could see the indignity. I could see this humiliation of this man who was an assimilated-- an-- he was 100% assimilated Jew. 

Yeah, I saw-- and I-- first time-- and I looked at him. I-- I already regretted that I came back before because of that band. I went with him to his room. I told him the story. And I showed him what I have written, two or three pages. 

And I was waiting that he should congratulate me on my writing. And I could see that something is wrong. He looked above me away like you would-- like he was embarrassed-- like he was embarrassed-- like he was embarrassed to look at me. I could see that there is something wrong. 

In the end, he said, look, I must congratulate you. You wrote an excellent appeal. Your German language is perfect. And your argument, he told me, you could have-- you could have done it in-- in Berlin or in German or-- or-- or in Vienna. But the trouble is that the court of-- the old court of Vienna doesn't exist anymore. You are playing with fire. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
